design_philosophy.core_loop: Collect five treasures across open-world levels using Scrooge's cane-based movement (ground swing, pogo-stick jump) to overcome platforming challenges and defeat boss guardians
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Exploration vs. resource management: player can visit levels in any order but must balance health recovery costs ($3M per use) against treasure collection
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Embodying Scrooge McDuck as a globe-trotting treasure hunter, solving level-specific puzzles and confronting themed bosses across distinct exotic locations (African Mines, Amazon, Himalayas, Transylvania, Moon)
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Rejects linear progression in favor of non-linear level selection; mechanically ties narrative outcomes (three endings) directly to financial performance; implements permadeath-adjacent design via expensive health restoration; emphasizes precise platforming and pogo-stick mechanics as core identity rather than narrative delivery

difficulty.lives_system: Health points system with ice cream pickups for restoration; Select button cheat restores full HP for $3,000,000 in-game currency
difficulty.checkpointing: Per-level checkpoint system; player returns to level start on death, no mid-level saves
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High: player can choose level order, manage health spending strategically, and deliberately pursue bad ending ($0 completion) through controlled spending

progression.upgrade_system: Cumulative treasure collection ($1M per boss defeated, 5 main treasures = $5M base; two secret treasures hidden across all levels); diamond pickups increase fortune
progression.unlock_mechanism: Certain level areas require items obtained from NPC characters (e.g., King of Terra Fermies provides key to progress in African Mines); non-linear level access means revisiting locations necessary for essential progression items
replayability_hooks: Three distinct endings tied to final money amount: regular (any completion), great (≥$10M), bad ($0)
Secret treasures hidden across levels encourage thorough exploration
Multiple level paths and NPC interactions vary per playthrough
Speedrun categories including 'Pacifist' and 'Best Ending' suggest challenge-run communities

world_building_techniques: Level theming tied to DuckTales cartoon IP (recognizable locations: African Mines, Amazon jungle, Himalayas, Transylvania castle, Moon base)
NPC character appearances from show (Launchpad, Gizmoduck, Louie, Terra Fermies) provide context and mechanical gating
Boss designs reflect thematic enemies (Dracula Duck in Transylvania, themed guardians per level)
Ending screens contextualize outcome narratively (bad ending shows Scrooge 'losing fortune')
Title screen and minimal in-game text; narrative delivered through gameplay context rather than exposition

boss_design.philosophy: Pattern-based encounters with RNG manipulation; each level contains one treasure-guarding boss; final sequence adds two antagonists (Dracula Duck, then Flintheart Glomgold + Magica DeSpell)
notable_bosses: Boss 2 (African Mines guardian): RNG-dependent transformation and flight patterns (multiple action states: top/middle/low flight)
Boss 3 (jungle/mountain level): exhibits variable Y-speed and rolling duration, suggesting physics-based or adaptive difficulty
Dracula Duck (final): requires collection of all five treasures before challenge; serves as narrative climax
Flintheart Glomgold & Magica DeSpell (post-final): secondary antagonists encountered after Dracula Duck defeat

faction_indicators: Speedrun community divisions: 'Easy' vs. 'Difficult' self-reported difficulty tiers in runs; suggests skill expression and routing debates
Category splits (Any%, Pacifist, Best Ending, Bad Ending) indicate community-driven challenge definitions
danger_signals: RNG-dependent boss behavior (Boss 2/3 patterns) creates friction in speedrunning—documented frame-perfect manipulation techniques on TASVideos indicate design fragility under TAS conditions
Pogo-stick physics precision creates execution barriers; regional PAL version required speed boost compensation, hinting at frame-dependent difficulty balancing
environmental_storytelling: Level-specific hazards mirror location themes (lava in mines, water/vines in Amazon, ice in Himalayas, coffins/spikes in Transylvania, gravity on Moon)
NPC placement and dialogue gating indicate social puzzle-solving (obtain key from King to progress)
Treasure chest and diamond distribution guides player exploration toward 'wealthy' vs. 'poor' playstyles

sources_used: speedrun (speedrun.com leaderboards for difficulty metadata and category structure)
tasvideos (RNG mechanics, boss pattern documentation, frame-perfect techniques)
wikipedia (gameplay mechanics, level structure, narrative endings, development team, character roles)
tcrf (regional differences, unused content, debug features, ending conditions)
